content.text: Systematic debugging is the practice of moving from an observed symptom — an agent stuck in a loop, a tool call that never fires, a response that contradicts the provided data — to a verified root cause using repeatable techniques rather than intuition. In agentic AI systems, this is harder than in conventional software: the control flow spans async I/O, a nondeterministic LLM, external tool APIs, and a stateful session store, all executing concurrently.
What it is

Debugging in the context of agentic AI is the discipline of isolating which component in the agent stack — the orchestrator, the LLM, a specific tool, or the underlying infrastructure — is responsible for incorrect or unexpected behavior. The techniques borrow from classical software debugging (breakpoints, logging, minimal reproduction) but extend them to account for properties unique to agent systems: nondeterministic model outputs, multi-turn conversational state, and side-effecting tool calls.

An agent debugging session typically follows a five-step pattern: observe the symptom in production logs or an evaluation run; capture the run's trace and raw payloads; reproduce the failure in isolation; bisect the causal layer; and then fix, verify, and promote the fix as a regression test. Each step maps to a specific technique covered in this document.

Unlike traditional service debugging, the LLM layer is opaque and stochastic. This forces a distinction between bugs that are deterministic — a Python exception, a missing API key, a state machine that never transitions — and probabilistic failures — the model occasionally picks the wrong tool or hallucinates a parameter name. The diagnostic approach differs sharply for each.
Why it matters

Production agent incidents rarely present with a stack trace. The failure surface looks like 'the bot gave a wrong answer' or 'the agent stopped responding' — descriptions that could originate from any of five distinct layers. Without a structured diagnostic method, engineers fall back on prompt tweaking: they change the system prompt, redeploy, and hope the symptom disappears. This loop is slow, unreliable, and risks introducing new failures while masking the original root cause.

Structured debugging reduces mean time to resolution (MTTR) by cutting out the guessing phase. When every run emits a unique run_id, every LLM request is logged with its raw payload, and every tool invocation records its arguments and return value, an engineer can reconstruct exactly what happened in under five minutes. Contrast this with systems that log only 'agent completed' — in those systems, diagnosing a latency regression or a tool misfire requires adding instrumentation, redeploying, and waiting for the issue to recur.

Beyond incident response, good debugging practices create a feedback loop. A repro script written to isolate a bug becomes a regression test. A trace captured during a quality failure becomes training data or an eval case. The investment in debuggability compounds over time.
Benefits
Faster root-cause isolation
— layered diagnosis eliminates entire sub-systems at once, avoiding the combinatorial search space of 'could be anything'
Reproducibility
— minimal repro scripts turn ephemeral failures into deterministic test cases that can be run in CI without a live LLM
Evidence-based fixes
— raw payload dumps and traces tell you what the model actually received, not what you think you sent
Regression prevention
— every fixed bug translated into a test means the same failure cannot silently return after a model upgrade or prompt edit
Cross-layer attribution
— clear records of which layer failed informs whether to file a bug against your orchestration code, your tool implementation, or your prompt engineering
Safer production changes
— understanding the exact failure mode before patching prevents fixes that address the symptom while leaving the underlying cause intact
Trade-offs
LLM nondeterminism
— even with temperature=0, models can produce different outputs across API versions, batching changes, or hardware migrations, making exact reproduction sometimes impossible without recorded payloads
Heisenbugs in async agents
— attaching a debugger or adding verbose logging can change timing, masking race conditions that exist only at production throughput
Secret exposure risk
— debug payloads contain raw LLM messages that may include user PII or API credentials passed as tool context; scrubbing is mandatory before storage or sharing
Overhead of instrumentation
— storing full request/response payloads for every LLM call can be 10–100x the size of structured log lines; retention policies and sampling are required at scale
Multi-turn complexity
— a bug may only manifest after a specific sequence of turns; isolating the minimal reproduction can require replaying large conversation histories
False attribution
— in multi-agent systems a downstream agent's error may surface in an upstream orchestrator's logs, sending the investigation to the wrong codebase
How it works
The five-layer diagnostic model

Every agentic system has a natural debugging hierarchy. Starting from the outermost observable layer and working inward saves time because outer-layer failures (network errors, missing credentials, quota exhaustion) are far more common than inner-layer failures (model regression, prompt ambiguity).
Infrastructure
— is the process even running? Check CPU, memory, worker crash logs, queue depth, and database connectivity before touching any application code
API / HTTP
— is the LLM API reachable? Check HTTP status codes, auth headers, rate limit responses (429), and request body validation errors (400)
Orchestrator
— is the state machine advancing? Check step count, max_iterations guard, state transitions, and session persistence
LLM
— what did the model actually receive and return? Inspect the messages array sent, the tool_calls JSON returned, finish_reason, and token counts
Tools
— when the model calls a tool, did the call succeed? Check argument validation, timeout expiry, permissions, and return value shape
Breakpoint-based debugging

Python's built-in debugger is effective for deterministic paths: state transitions, argument parsing, tool dispatch logic. The standard breakpoint() call (Python 3.7+) invokes whatever debugger is configured via PYTHONBREAKPOINT, defaulting to pdb. For async code running in an event loop, debugpy provides VS Code-compatible remote debugging that does not block other coroutines while paused.
Trace replay

Trace replay captures the exact LLM request and response payloads from a production failure, then replays them in a controlled environment using either a real LLM call or a mock that returns the recorded response. This decouples the investigation from live traffic and allows deterministic reproduction of nondeterministic failures. The key constraint is that replayed LLM responses must be raw JSON from the provider API, not reconstructed from application-level summaries.
Git bisect for prompt and code regressions

When a metric degrades after a release — eval pass rate drops, latency increases, a tool call starts being skipped — git bisect combined with an automated evaluation script can pinpoint the exact commit that introduced the regression. The evaluation script acts as the bisect oracle, returning exit code 0 for 'good' and 1 for 'bad'.
Minimal reproduction

A minimal reproduction script exercises the smallest possible path through the system that still triggers the failure. It typically replaces all external dependencies (search APIs, databases, LLMs in non-LLM-related bugs) with mocks or fixtures, and uses a hard-coded input rather than a user-facing endpoint. Converting a production failure into a minimal repro is often the highest-leverage step in the debugging process: it validates the hypothesis and produces a test case simultaneously.
Architecture

The diagram below shows the five debugging layers of a typical agentic AI system and the observation points at each transition. Debug hooks (dashed purple boxes) indicate where instrumentation should be placed to capture state for later analysis.

attrs.alt: Agentic AI layered debugging architecture diagram

content.text: Each data-flow arrow is a potential failure point. The orchestrator dispatches to the LLM, which returns tool_calls that the tool executor resolves against external services, then returns observations back up the chain. Debugging means identifying which arrow carries incorrect data. External services (APIs, databases, file systems) attach at the tool executor level and are treated as opaque dependencies that can only be observed through the tool's return value and any error it raises.
When to use it
Different debugging techniques apply to different failure modes. Choosing the right tool first prevents wasted effort.
Use a debugger (pdb/debugpy)
when the failure is in deterministic code: a parser exception, a state machine that skips a transition, an argument validation error in a tool, or an async task that hangs before making the LLM call
Use trace replay

when the failure involves LLM behavior: the model selected the wrong tool, generated malformed JSON, or ignored a constraint in the system prompt — replay the exact messages to confirm the model is the variable
Use minimal repro + mocks
when a bug only surfaces with a specific combination of tool outputs; mock the tools to return those values deterministically without depending on live external services
Use git bisect + evals
when a metric regressed between two releases and the diff is large enough that manual inspection is impractical
Use structured log search
as the first step for any production incident — filter by run_id, find the failing step, then decide which technique to apply for the causal investigation
Avoid live debugging in production
with breakpoints or full-payload logging at INFO level; use sampling, shadow traffic, or a staging replay environment instead
How to implement
Step 1 — assign run_id and emit entry/exit logs
Every agent invocation must generate a unique identifier that flows through all downstream calls. The run_id is the primary key for filtering logs, joining traces, and constructing repro scripts.

import uuid
import logging
import json
import time
from functools import wraps

logger = logging.getLogger("agent")

def run_id_context(func):
    """Decorator: attach run_id to every log call within an agent turn."""
    @wraps(func)
    async def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        run_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
        # Use a context var so nested calls inherit it
        import contextvars
        _run_id: contextvars.ContextVar[str] = contextvars.ContextVar("run_id", default="-")
        token = _run_id.set(run_id)
        start = time.monotonic()
        logger.info(json.dumps({"event": "run.start", "run_id": run_id}))
        try:
            result = await func(*args, run_id=run_id, **kwargs)
            logger.info(json.dumps({
                "event": "run.end",
                "run_id": run_id,
                "latency_ms": round((time.monotonic() - start) * 1000),
            }))
            return result
        except Exception as exc:
            logger.error(json.dumps({
                "event": "run.error",
                "run_id": run_id,
                "error": type(exc).__name__,
                "detail": str(exc),
            }))
            raise
        finally:
            _run_id.reset(token)
    return wrapper
Step 2 — log LLM request and response payloads at DEBUG

At INFO level log only summary fields (model, token counts, finish_reason). At DEBUG level log the full messages array and tool_calls so they can be retrieved for trace replay without polluting production log volumes.

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def call_llm_with_trace(messages: list, tools: list, run_id: str) -> dict:
    """Wrap LLM call with structured before/after logging."""
    logger.debug(json.dumps({
        "event": "llm.request",
        "run_id": run_id,
        "message_count": len(messages),
        # Only emit at DEBUG — contains raw prompts
        "messages": messages,
        "tools": [t["name"] for t in tools],
    }))

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=4096,
        messages=messages,
        tools=tools,
    )

    payload = response.model_dump()
    logger.info(json.dumps({
        "event": "llm.response",
        "run_id": run_id,
        "stop_reason": payload["stop_reason"],
        "input_tokens": payload["usage"]["input_tokens"],
        "output_tokens": payload["usage"]["output_tokens"],
    }))
    logger.debug(json.dumps({"event": "llm.response.full", "run_id": run_id, "payload": payload}))

    return payload
Step 3 — instrument the tool executor

import asyncio

async def execute_tool(tool_name: str, args: dict, run_id: str, registry: dict):
    """Dispatch a tool call with timing and error capture."""
    logger.info(json.dumps({
        "event": "tool.call",
        "run_id": run_id,
        "tool": tool_name,
        "args_keys": list(args.keys()),
    }))
    start = asyncio.get_event_loop().time()
    try:
        fn = registry[tool_name]
        result = await asyncio.wait_for(fn(**args), timeout=10.0)
        logger.info(json.dumps({
            "event": "tool.result",
            "run_id": run_id,
            "tool": tool_name,
            "latency_ms": round((asyncio.get_event_loop().time() - start) * 1000),
            "result_type": type(result).__name__,
        }))
        return result
    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
        logger.error(json.dumps({"event": "tool.timeout", "run_id": run_id, "tool": tool_name}))
        raise
    except Exception as exc:
        logger.error(json.dumps({
            "event": "tool.error",
            "run_id": run_id,
            "tool": tool_name,
            "error": type(exc).__name__,
            "detail": str(exc),
        }))
        raise
Step 4 — write a repro script from a captured run_id

# scripts/repro.py
# Usage: python scripts/repro.py --run-id <run_id> [--mock-llm]
import argparse
import asyncio
import json
from pathlib import Path

def load_trace(run_id: str) -> dict:
    """Load the captured LLM request/response for a run from cold storage."""
    path = Path(f"traces/{run_id}.json")
    return json.loads(path.read_text())

async def replay(run_id: str, mock_llm: bool):
    trace = load_trace(run_id)
    messages = trace["messages"]
    tools = trace["tools"]

    if mock_llm:
        # Return the recorded response deterministically
        llm_response = trace["llm_response"]
    else:
        import anthropic
        client = anthropic.Anthropic()
        llm_response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=4096,
            messages=messages,
            tools=tools,
        ).model_dump()

    print(json.dumps(llm_response, indent=2))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--run-id", required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--mock-llm", action="store_true")
    args = parser.parse_args()
    asyncio.run(replay(args.run_id, args.mock_llm))
Examples
Example 1 — interactive breakpoint debugging

The following example demonstrates setting a breakpoint inside an async orchestrator loop to inspect the model's tool_calls before the tool executor dispatches them. It uses debugpy for async-compatible debugging from VS Code.
Example 2 — automated trace capture and replay

This example shows a complete trace capture and replay harness. The first run captures all LLM payloads to disk. A subsequent replay run uses the recorded response, producing deterministic output without an API call.

# Requires: pip install anthropic
import asyncio
import json
import uuid
from pathlib import Path

import anthropic

TRACE_DIR = Path("./traces")
TRACE_DIR.mkdir(exist_ok=True)

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

TOOLS = [
    {
        "name": "search",
        "description": "Search the web.",
        "input_schema": {
            "type": "object",
            "properties": {"query": {"type": "string"}},
            "required": ["query"],
        },
    }
]

def mock_search(query: str) -> str:
    return f"Search results for '{query}': [result1, result2]"

async def agent_turn(
    user_message: str,
    run_id: str,
    recorded_response: dict | None = None,
) -> str:
    """Execute one agent turn; record or replay the LLM response."""
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": user_message}]

    if recorded_response is None:
        # Live mode: call the API and record the response
        raw = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=1024,
            tools=TOOLS,
            messages=messages,
        ).model_dump()
        trace_path = TRACE_DIR / f"{run_id}.json"
        trace_path.write_text(json.dumps({
            "run_id": run_id,
            "messages": messages,
            "tools": TOOLS,
            "llm_response": raw,
        }, indent=2))
        print(f"Trace saved to {trace_path}")
    else:
        # Replay mode: use the recorded response
        raw = recorded_response
        print("Using recorded LLM response (no API call)")

    stop_reason = raw.get("stop_reason")
    if stop_reason == "end_turn":
        for block in raw.get("content", []):
            if block.get("type") == "text":
                return block["text"]

    if stop_reason == "tool_use":
        for block in raw.get("content", []):
            if block.get("type") == "tool_use":
                tool_name = block["name"]
                tool_input = block["input"]
                print(f"Tool called: {tool_name}({tool_input})")
                result = mock_search(tool_input["query"])
                return f"Tool result: {result}"

    return "No usable response"


async def main():
    run_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    print(f"run_id: {run_id}")

    # First pass: live
    answer = await agent_turn("What is the capital of France?", run_id)
    print(f"Answer: {answer}")

    # Second pass: replay same run
    trace = json.loads((TRACE_DIR / f"{run_id}.json").read_text())
    replay_answer = await agent_turn(
        "What is the capital of France?",
        run_id + "-replay",
        recorded_response=trace["llm_response"],
    )
    print(f"Replay answer: {replay_answer}")
    assert answer == replay_answer, "Replay produced different output!"
    print("Replay matched live run.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())
Real-world example

A customer support agent at a SaaS company stops calling the order_lookup tool on approximately 15% of queries. Users receive generic responses instead of order-specific answers. The failure appears non-deterministic and cannot be reproduced in staging.
The investigation proceeds in layers:
Infrastructure check
— worker health dashboards show no anomalies; all instances are responding normally
HTTP/API check
— LLM API response times and error rates are within SLA; no 429 or 500 responses in the timeframe
Log filter by symptom

— querying structured logs for tool='order_lookup' AND event='tool.call' reveals calls are being logged for only 85% of order-related queries; the missing 15% show finish_reason='end_turn' before any tool call
Trace comparison

— extracting two raw LLM payloads: one where order_lookup was called and one where it was not. Diff reveals that failing requests have a messages array 40% longer due to accumulated conversation history, pushing the order number past the model's effective attention window in the user message
Root cause

— the system prompt includes an instruction to call order_lookup when an order number is present. For long conversations the model reliably follows this instruction. But when the user's order number appears in a message that is now truncated at the token limit in the effective context, the instruction is never triggered
Fix

— extract the order number during message ingestion and inject it as a structured field in the system prompt rather than relying on the model to detect it in long conversation history. Add an integration test that asserts order_lookup is called when an order number is present, regardless of conversation length

The total investigation time from symptom to root cause was 45 minutes, driven entirely by the structured log filter and trace comparison. Without those payloads, the team would have started with prompt tweaks.
Common mistakes
Changing multiple variables at once
Updating the model version, the system prompt, and a tool definition simultaneously makes it impossible to attribute a behavior change to any single cause. Always change one variable per experiment.

# WRONG: simultaneous changes obscure causation
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",        # changed model
    system="Updated system prompt",  # changed prompt
    messages=messages,
    tools=updated_tools,              # changed tools
)

# RIGHT: change one variable; hold the others fixed
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",  # baseline model
    system=ORIGINAL_SYSTEM_PROMPT,       # unchanged
    messages=messages,
    tools=ORIGINAL_TOOLS,                # unchanged
)
Debugging in production without sampling

Enabling DEBUG-level LLM payload logging in production at 100% of traffic can produce log volumes that saturate the log pipeline, mask the very errors being investigated, and expose PII in log storage. Use a sampling flag or replay in staging.
Assuming the model is the bug

The most common incorrect hypothesis is 'the model hallucinated' or 'the model ignored the instruction'. Outer layers — missing credentials, malformed tool schema, context truncation — explain the majority of apparent model failures. Exhaust the deterministic layers before concluding the model is at fault.
Ignoring async cancellation

An agent that 'hangs' in production is frequently waiting on a task that was cancelled at the HTTP layer but whose coroutine was never cleaned up. Check for asyncio.CancelledError handlers and unclosed aiohttp or httpx clients.
Best practices
run_id is mandatory
— every agent invocation must generate and propagate a unique run_id through all log lines, API responses, and stored traces; without it, multi-step debugging is impossible in production
Log at the layer boundary
— emit a log record immediately before and after each major hand-off: request→LLM, LLM→tool, tool→orchestrator; this triangulates failures to a single boundary
Store full payloads at DEBUG, summaries at INFO
— the DEBUG level is disabled by default in production but can be enabled for a single run_id in staging replay without changing global log verbosity
Redact before storing
— strip API keys, OAuth tokens, and personal identifiers from payloads before writing to cold storage; use a scrubber that runs at the serialization boundary
Build repro scripts from every incident
— a repro script is the fastest path from trace to regression test; budget 20 minutes per incident for this step
Version your prompts alongside your code
— store system prompts in version-controlled files so git bisect can operate on them when a quality regression is introduced by a prompt edit
Run evals after every model upgrade
— switching model versions is effectively a dependency upgrade; treat it with the same discipline as a library major version bump
Performance & scalability

Instrumentation that is correct but slow creates a different class of bug. Synchronous log writes inside an async agent loop add latency on every step. The following practices keep debugging overhead sub-millisecond at scale.
Async log handlers
— use QueueHandler + QueueListener from Python's logging module to write log records off the hot path; this prevents disk I/O from blocking the event loop
Sampling for payload capture
— at 1,000 agent runs per minute, storing full LLM payloads for every run produces ~50 GB/day at average prompt sizes; sample 1–5% or store only on error
Index by run_id only
— avoid full-text search on raw payload content in log storage; index only structured fields (run_id, step, tool name, error code) and retrieve the payload by primary key
Trace TTL
— retain full traces for 30 days for incident investigation; retain structured log summaries for 90–180 days for trend analysis; delete raw payloads on schedule to control storage cost
Lazy serialization
— serialize the payload to JSON only if the log level is actually enabled; avoid building expensive debug strings that are discarded by the log filter
Tooling & ecosystem
Python debugging tools
pdb
— built-in, zero-dependency debugger; type
c
(continue),
n
(next),
p var
(print) in the interactive prompt
debugpy
— VS Code / PyCharm compatible remote debugger; supports async coroutines without blocking the event loop
icecream (ic)
— drop-in replacement for print() that includes file, line number, and variable name in output; install with
pip install icecream
pytest --pdb
— drops into pdb on the first test failure; combine with
pytest -x
to stop at the first error
Observability platforms
Langfuse
— open-source LLM observability; records traces, token costs, and eval scores; self-hostable
LangSmith
— LangChain's tracing and evaluation platform; stores full request/response pairs with run hierarchy
Arize Phoenix
— open-source LLM tracing with OpenTelemetry instrumentation; integrates with any Python LLM client via a one-line instrumentor call
Helicone
— proxy-based LLM observability; intercepts all calls through an HTTP proxy so no code changes are required

# Arize Phoenix: instrument all Anthropic calls automatically
import phoenix as px
from openinference.instrumentation.anthropic import AnthropicInstrumentor

px.launch_app()
AnthropicInstrumentor().instrument()

# All subsequent client.messages.create() calls are now traced automatically
import anthropic
client = anthropic.Anthropic()
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=256,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Hello"}],
)
print(response.content[0].text)
# Open http://localhost:6006 to see the trace in the Phoenix UI
Git bisect with an eval oracle

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# scripts/bisect_oracle.sh — used as: git bisect run scripts/bisect_oracle.sh

# Install dependencies silently
pip install -q -r requirements.txt

# Run the evaluation suite; exit 0 = good, 1 = bad
python -m pytest tests/evals/tool_selection_test.py --tb=no -q

# Bisect session
git bisect start
git bisect bad                  # current HEAD fails evals
git bisect good v2.3.0          # last known good release
git bisect run scripts/bisect_oracle.sh
# git automatically checks out commits and runs the oracle until the first bad commit is found
Comparison with alternatives
The table below compares the four primary debugging approaches for agentic systems across key dimensions.
Technique
Best for
Reproducibility
LLM coverage
Production safe?
Interactive debugger (pdb/debugpy)
Deterministic code paths: parsers, state machines, tool logic
Exact — same code, same result
No — cannot step into LLM inference
No — blocks the event loop
Trace replay (recorded payloads)
LLM behavior: wrong tool choice, bad JSON, ignored constraints
High — same messages produce same response
Yes — replays actual model behavior
Yes — runs in staging
Minimal repro + mocks
Tool interaction bugs, orchestrator logic with specific tool outputs
Exact — mocks return fixed values
Partial — real or mock LLM
Yes — no live traffic
Git bisect + evals
Prompt or code regressions across commits
Exact — deterministic eval script
Yes — eval covers model behavior
Yes — runs on historical commits
Log-based search (run_id filter)
Production incidents, first step of any investigation
Low — logs only capture what was logged
Partial — depends on payload logging
Yes — passive observation
Security & reliability
Secrets in debug payloads

LLM messages frequently contain API credentials, OAuth tokens, or personally identifiable information that users include in their queries. A debug payload that captures the full messages array captures this data verbatim. Before writing any payload to log storage or a trace database, run a scrubber that replaces known secret patterns and known PII patterns with placeholder strings.

import re

# Patterns to redact before logging
REDACT_PATTERNS = [
    (re.compile(r"sk-[A-Za-z0-9]{40,}"), "<sk-REDACTED>"),
    (r"Bearer [A-Za-z0-9\-._~+/]+=*", "Bearer <REDACTED>"),
    (re.compile(r"\b[A-Z0-9]{20}\b"), "<ACCESS-KEY-REDACTED>"),
]

def scrub(text: str) -> str:
    for pattern, replacement in REDACT_PATTERNS:
        if isinstance(pattern, str):
            pattern = re.compile(pattern)
        text = pattern.sub(replacement, text)
    return text

def scrub_payload(payload: dict) -> dict:
    """Deep-scrub a serialized LLM payload before logging or storing."""
    serialized = scrub(str(payload))
    # Re-parse only if needed; for storage, the scrubbed string is sufficient
    return serialized
Reliability of debug instrumentation

Debug instrumentation that can throw must not be allowed to crash the agent. Wrap all observability calls in try/except blocks that log the instrumentation error at WARNING level and continue execution. A failed trace write is preferable to a failed agent run.
Access control on trace storage

Trace databases contain raw LLM messages that may be more sensitive than the application data the agent acts on. Apply the same access controls as production databases: role-based access, audit logging of who retrieved which run_id, and data retention limits enforced by automated deletion jobs.
Key takeaways
Debug in layers from outermost to innermost: infrastructure, HTTP/API, orchestrator, LLM, tools — eliminate each before moving inward
Every agent run must emit a unique run_id that flows through all logs, traces, and API responses; it is the primary key for all investigation
Use interactive debuggers for deterministic code; use trace replay with recorded payloads for nondeterministic LLM behavior
Store full LLM request/response payloads at DEBUG level only, sampled in production; always scrub secrets and PII before persisting
Convert every fixed bug into a regression test — either a unit test for deterministic paths or an eval test for LLM-behavioral ones
Change one variable per debugging experiment: model version, system prompt, tool definition, or orchestrator logic — never more than one
Use git bisect with an automated eval oracle to pinpoint regressions in large diffs rather than reviewing commits manually
Instrumentation that can throw must never crash the agent; wrap all observability calls with try/except and continue execution on failure
Multi-agent systems require end-to-end trace correlation; every sub-agent call must inherit and propagate the parent's run_id
